title: How the parietal cortex loses body ownership during severe dissociation
author: Kalesh
excerpt:
You look down at your hand and it doesn't feel like yours 

 I remember the first time I looked at my own arm and felt absolutely nothing. Not numbness exactly...
metaDescription:
You look down at your hand and it doesn't feel like yours 

 I remember the first time I looked at my own arm and felt absolutely nothing. Not numbness ex...
htmlContent:
You look down at your hand and it doesn't feel like yours

I remember the first time I looked at my own arm and felt absolutely nothing. Not numbness exactly. Something stranger. My hand was there, resting on the table, but it belonged to someone else. Or maybe it was a prop. A fake hand placed there by some stagehand I couldn't see. I could move it. I knew it was attached to my body. But the feeling of mine was gone.

That's dissociation. Not just spacing out during a boring meeting. I'm talking about the kind where your body becomes a stranger. Where you watch yourself from a distance, or from inside a glass box, or from somewhere so far back in your own skull that you're not sure you're really there at all.

Here's the thing. This isn't a spiritual experience. It's not a sign that you're "too sensitive" or "not grounded enough." It's neuroscience. Hard, physical, measurable brain activity. And one of the key players in this bizarre experience is a region you've probably never heard of: the parietal cortex.

Let me walk you through what happens when your brain stops claiming your own body as yours. Stay with me here. It gets weird, but it also starts to make a terrible kind of sense.

The parietal cortex is your body's real estate agent

Think of the parietal cortex as the part of your brain that handles the paperwork of embodiment. It's located roughly at the top and back of your head, near where your skull curves. And its job is to integrate information from your senses, your muscles, your inner organs, and your environment into a single coherent picture: this body, here, now, mine.

That's not a simple task. Your brain is constantly receiving streams of data. Touch signals from your skin. Proprioceptive signals from your joints telling you where your limbs are in space. Visual information about what you're seeing. Auditory cues. Even signals from your gut and heart. The parietal cortex has to weave all of that together into a unified experience. When it works properly, you don't notice it. You just are your body.

But here's what researchers like Peter Levine and Bessel van der Kolk have spent decades showing us. When trauma overwhelms the nervous system, the brain starts making emergency decisions. One of those decisions can be to cut the connection between the parietal cortex and the incoming sensory data. Why? Because feeling your body during a traumatic event is unbearable. The pain, the terror, the helplessness. It's too much. So the brain does what it can to survive. It disconnects you from your own physical experience.

And once that connection is severed, even temporarily, your body stops feeling like yours.

Think about it. If the parietal cortex is the real estate agent who says "this property belongs to you," dissociation is the moment the agent quits. Suddenly you're standing in a house that looks familiar but has no emotional weight. You don't live here anymore. You're just visiting.

If you want to understand this from a practical perspective, a resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy. It gives you a framework for why your nervous system makes these emergency decisions in the first place.

What the research actually shows about body ownership and the parietal cortex

There's a famous experiment called the rubber hand illusion that demonstrates this perfectly. You sit with one hand hidden behind a screen. A fake rubber hand is placed in front of you. The researcher strokes both your real hand and the rubber hand simultaneously with a brush. After about thirty seconds, most people start to feel the rubber hand as their own. They flinch when it's threatened. They report that it genuinely feels like part of their body.

That's the parietal cortex being tricked. It's integrating the visual information (I see a hand being stroked) with the tactile information (I feel a hand being stroked) and concluding: that rubber hand must be mine.

Now flip that. What happens when the parietal cortex stops integrating altogether? You get the opposite effect. Your actual hand, the one attached to your body, stops feeling like it belongs to you. The integration fails. The sense of ownership collapses.

This is exactly what happens in severe dissociation. Research using fMRI scans shows that people with dissociative disorders have altered activity in the parietal cortex, particularly in the right temporoparietal junction. That's a small region near where the temporal and parietal lobes meet. And it's critical for distinguishing self from other, for knowing where your body ends and the world begins.

When this area goes quiet during dissociation, the boundary dissolves. You might feel like you're floating above yourself. Or like your body is a shell you're trapped inside. Or like you're watching a movie of your own life. The parietal cortex has stopped doing its job of saying "this is yours."

Wild, right? Your sense of being a person, of having a body that belongs to you, depends on a few grams of neural tissue doing its job. And when trauma hijacks that system, you don't just feel disconnected. You are disconnected. Neurologically.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry. It helps bridge the gap between the neuroscience and the lived experience of feeling like you've lost yourself.

Why your nervous system makes this brutal trade

Here's the thing that people who haven't experienced severe dissociation don't understand. It's not a bug. It's a feature. A horrifying, desperate feature.

Your nervous system, specifically your vagus nerve and the polyvagal pathways that Stephen Porges mapped out, is constantly scanning for safety and danger. When it detects a threat that you cannot escape, fight, or even freeze effectively, it has one last option. It can shut down your connection to your body entirely. This is called the dorsal vagal collapse, or what some researchers call the "shutdown" state.

In that state, the parietal cortex receives less input. It stops getting the rich stream of sensory data it needs to maintain the feeling of body ownership. And without that data, it can't do its job. You go offline. You become a spectator in your own life.

I know this because I've lived it. And I know it because I've read the research. Janina Fisher, who worked closely with Bessel van der Kolk, describes dissociation as the "soul's way of surviving what the body cannot bear." She's right. But the cost is enormous.

The cost is that you lose your sense of being real. You lose the feeling of being embodied, of being present, of being alive in any meaningful way. The parietal cortex doesn't just process body ownership. It's involved in the sense of agency, the feeling that you are the one moving your hand, making your decisions, living your life. When that goes, you're adrift.

If you are curious about inherited trauma patterns, you might find value in It Didn't Start with You by Mark Wolynn. It explores how trauma gets passed down and how it shapes your nervous system before you even have words for it.

You can rebuild the connection, but it takes time

I want to be honest with you. There's no quick fix for this. You can't just "ground yourself" and expect the parietal cortex to start working again if your nervous system is still in survival mode. The wellness industry loves to tell you to breathe deeply and feel your feet on the floor. But if your brain has learned that feeling your body is dangerous, those techniques can actually make dissociation worse.

What works is slower. It's gentler. It's about creating enough safety in your nervous system that the parietal cortex can slowly, cautiously, start re-integrating sensory data again. This might mean working with a therapist who understands structural dissociation. It might mean somatic experiencing with someone who knows not to push you too fast. It might mean tiny, almost invisible experiments in body awareness that last only a few seconds at a time.

Here's what I've learned. The parietal cortex is plastic. It can change. It can learn new patterns. But it needs your nervous system to signal that it's safe enough to come back online. That takes time. That takes patience. That takes accepting that you may not feel fully embodied for a while, and that's okay.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No by Gabor Mate. It will help you understand why your body made this choice in the first place, and how to start making peace with that decision.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did you pull back? Did you feel a flicker of recognition? Did you go numb? All of that is data. All of it is your nervous system talking to you. You don't have to fix it right now. You just have to notice.

The parietal cortex will come back. Not all at once. Not on command. But piece by piece, as your nervous system learns that it's safe to be here, in this body, in this moment. That's the work. And it's worth doing.

Healing Journey Resources

  • When the Body Says No by Gabor Mate
  • What Happened to You? by Oprah Winfrey and Bruce Perry
  • It Didn't Start with You by Mark Wolynn
  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy by Deb Dana
